author: maintainer-agent

patch.value:
pm restore <id> <target> can rewrite an item back to a prior version, but there is no READ-ONLY way to inspect what an item looked like at a given version or timestamp. Agents auditing provenance today must either run a mutating restore (then restore back) or manually fold JSON Patch entries from pm history --full — expensive, error-prone, and impossible to compose in pipelines.

Proposal: a non-mutating projection flag on the read path:
- pm get <id> --at <version|ISO timestamp> reconstructs the item state by replaying the append-only history stream up to the target and renders it through the normal get projection (json/toon, --tree excluded).
- Output marks the projection clearly (as_of_version, as_of_timestamp, reconstructed: true) so downstream tooling can never confuse a historical view with current state.
- Reuses the same replay engine as pm restore (shared SDK primitive; no second replay implementation) so restore and read-projection can never diverge.
- SDK: expose as a first-class read primitive (getItemAt) so custom tools built on the SDK get time-travel reads for free — a prerequisite for building VCS-like systems on pm primitives.

This closes the read half of the durability story: the append-only hash-chained history already guarantees every version is recorded; this makes every version cheaply inspectable.
Point-in-time read projection: pm get --at <version|timestamp> renders reconstructed historical item state without mutating

pm get <id> --at <version> and --at <timestamp> return the reconstructed state with reconstructed:true and as_of_* fields in both json and toon; reconstruction reuses the restore replay helper (single implementation, verified by test); invalid/future targets fail with a structured error listing the valid version range; SDK exposes the same primitive with reference docs; works after history-compact (checkpoint-aware replay); no mutation, no lock, no history entry is written by the read

message: Long-horizon durability planning: read-only time-travel projection gap

patch.value:
pm restore <id> <target> can rewrite an item back to a prior version, but there is no READ-ONLY way to inspect what an item looked like at a given version or timestamp. Agents auditing provenance today must either run a mutating restore (then restore back) or manually fold JSON Patch entries from pm history --full — expensive, error-prone, and impossible to compose in pipelines.

Proposal: a non-mutating projection flag on the read path:
- pm get <id> --at <version|ISO timestamp> reconstructs the item state by replaying the append-only history stream up to the target and renders it through the normal get projection (json/toon, --tree excluded).
- Output marks the projection clearly (as_of_version, as_of_timestamp, reconstructed: true) so downstream tooling can never confuse a historical view with current state.
- Reuses the same replay engine as pm restore (shared SDK primitive; no second replay implementation) so restore and read-projection can never diverge.
- SDK: expose as a first-class read primitive (getItemAt) so custom tools built on the SDK get time-travel reads for free — a prerequisite for building VCS-like systems on pm primitives.

This closes the read half of the durability story: the append-only hash-chained history already guarantees every version is recorded; this makes every version cheaply inspectable.

## Scope note 2026-07-07 — workspace-level projection (future phase)
Item-scoped get --at is phase 1. A later phase can generalize the same reconstruction to workspace-level reads (list/context --at <timestamp>) for retrospectives and flow analysis over historical states; that phase must be index-backed (pm-801d) to avoid replaying every stream, and pairs naturally with the flow metrics in pm-pee5. Do not start it before item-scoped --at ships and the read index exists — filed as a scope note here (not a separate item) to keep one owner for time-travel reads.
